sentences.text: Then it's going to be OK.
They--
My name is Barbara Turkeltaub.
And before I was married, I was Barbara Batia Gurwicz.
G-U-R-W-I-C-Z.
It's a Hebrew name for Barbara.
At home, I was called Bachia.
It's a pet name for Barbara.
Vilna.
I was born in Vilna.
When I was born, in 1934, it was-- Vilna belonged to Poland.
But later on, it became capital city of Lithuania.
So-- and, of course, in history, before that, it was going from-- it belonged to Russia.
Then, belonged to Lithuania, then it belonged to Poland.
The history is full of-- of the happening for-- about Vilna.
I-- I don't know exactly, because, I'll tell you, we-- when I came out of convent, my mother wanted to get away fast from Vilna.
Then, when we came to Poland, to Lodz, from there, she wanted to get away fast again to safer place.
Well, every time she had somebody to do the papers for us-- so whoever thought the age that I am, that's what they put.
It's not only for me, for all our family, and a lot of other people.
So the discrepancy might be a month, a year maybe, not much more than that.
I don't.
I don't.
We-- I celebrate-- we all celebrate our birthday on Rosh Hashanah.
Right now?
And my sister's 60.
I have a brother also.
He was born in 1943.
That I know, even though I didn't witness.
But my mom kept talking about it, so I remember that.
Yes, certainly.
I lived near-- near a river, Vilia.
I remember the name of the-- of the river.
Our house was full with people.
That's my father.
I had two older sisters.
And my grandfather lived with us.
I had a younger sister, Leah, the one that lives now in Israel.
And it was a happy household.
My father was working for the city government.
My--
My-- my-- my Moses.
Yeah.
Moishe.
They didn't call him Moses.
You know, they call him Moishe.
So-- and it was a-- it was a happy household.
I didn't know that-- I didn't feel any different than any other people.
I had friends.
We played outside.
I was too young to go to school, but my two older sisters were going to Hebrew schools.
Hebrew was the familiar language for us.
So when-- later on, when we came to Israel, it wasn't-- it wasn't difficult for us.
Within months, we spoke fluently.
I speak fluently now.
So it was very familiar.
My mother comes from a very Orthodox family.
Mina.
Her name is Mina.
She comes-- her maiden name was Gimple.
And she comes from a family.
She had four brothers.
She had one sister that was a half-sister.
And, yeah, four brothers.
They all perished in Lithuania.
They all died.
Not very much.
I have seen my-- because they lived in Kovne, so they-- I remember one of my uncle's came with his son to Vilnius for a recital.
His younger son was a protege in violin, in music.
And so, he was-- they came.
And so they stay with us.
And then, several other times that I remember them.
I remember very well my grandmother.
She visited us frequently and stayed with us quite a bit.
And she really wasn't-- she was only 16 years older than my mother.
And not-- she was 16 years old when she had my mother.
Can you imagine?
This is absolutely-- how young at that time.
But they were very religious people and it was not uncommon.
So I remember her very well, and the fact that she didn't speak any other language except Lithuanian and Jewish.
So didn't speak Polish or Russian like we did.
Or Hebrew.
Go back to your parents for just a minute.
Your father, what did he do for a living?
He was an accountant and worked for--
Yes.
And my mom also had a little business going in her house.
She was-- she was a very good seamstress.
So she was teaching young women to sew.
And we had a large veranda, I remember.
A glass veranda, so there were quite a few sewing machines.
And she had all these women there, young women.
They stayed with us for about-- quite some time, for a year or two, until they were ready to go on their own.
Very comfortable, yes, but not-- not rich, but comfortable.
We had a-- we had a cook.
I remember because my mother was very kosher, and so we had a lady that-- her name was Dopcia.
I remember her so well.
Yes.
Because she would not let anybody into the kitchen.
She was a cook.
She was cooking for-- for all of us and was afraid that we would make trife the kitchen, so would not let anybody to the kitchen.
And then we had a-- we had a nanny.
And I remember her very well, a very dear, dear lady.
And her name was Anastasia.
Nascia, we called her.
I-- I-- I never remember our house without her.
And then, when war begin-- and, of course, she was not Jewish.
She was Russian Orthodox.
And so she-- she could-- you know, she didn't go to ghetto with us.
So, after the war, she seek us out anyway and found us, and lived with us.
We couldn't-- we were destitute.
We couldn't pay her or anything.
And actually, we didn't need any-- we didn't need a nanny after the war.
But she was with us.
She stayed with us until we left for Israel.
And my mother tried very hard to make papers for her so she can come with us to Israel, but it was very difficult.
And she couldn't, so she remained in Lodz.
But she was part of our family, really, and helped us during the war, while in ghetto, smuggled things, and arranged things.
Was in constant contact with my parents.
So she was godsend.
I remember her very lovingly.
And I am so sorry, I don't have any pictures of her.
But my sister-- see.
I-- my sister, younger sister that lives in Israel, she's only two years younger.
She doesn't remember anything.
She doesn't remember before the war.
She doesn't remember during the war.
And great part of after the war, she doesn't.
Her life begin when she came to Israel.
And that's what she remembers.
But one thing she does remember, she remembers Nascia, our nanny, that took care of us in very loving way.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I had older-- I had two older sisters.
Mira and-- and Hannah.
And-- it was a-- it was a great difference in ages.
So they were both together always.
And they were going to Hebrew school.
And I-- they didn't play very much with us, with me.
Didn't bother with me too much.
A little bit, but they were very busy.
They had very busy lives.
The older one was in already high school, Hebrew high school, in Vilna.
I remember-- that's another thing that I remember so vividly.
When war started, the-- the Nazis took the whole high school.
And they were marching these young people, not knowing where they're marching.
They were-- they were going to Ponary.
They were shot right there in Ponary, without even any kind of knowledge.
Years later, when my nephews were asking me-- my nephews in Israel, when they were asking me, how could you go like sheep.
How could you go like sheep?
Well, they didn't know where they were going.
The whole high school were taken and was-- and few German [NON-ENGLISH] with-- with machine guns were-- were directing them to the famous forest, Ponary.
Later on, it came to be known.
But at that time, it didn't.
Well, thank god my sister was not-- sisters were not in that group that were taken.
Somehow, they were-- managed to-- not to be in that group.
I don't know how.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later, when we were in ghetto, my father and they were-- because they were older, so they went to partisans.
That's how it was arranged.
Yes.
Yes, in 1936.
So-- so we were-- I-- somehow, I was always together with my sister.
I always took care of her.
I was-- and-- ours was very happy childhood.
But, really, all around the house, all around the house with Nascia, going to parks, going to different carnivals.
And it was a very happy childhood.
We even slept together.
We had a cradle that I can't figure out how big that had to be because both of us-- one on one side, the other at the other.
I remember the cradle was wrought iron with blue.
Probably from other kids.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the war started, I didn't know-- my sister certainly not.
I had no idea what's going on.
But, in the household, I-- I sense there was intensity.
There was awful feeling and I didn't understand what it was.
My father kept talking Germans, Germans, and Bolsheviks, and Bolsheviks.
And who are Germans?
Who were Bolsheviks?
It was beyond my understanding.
You were all of five years old?
If I didn't-- Yes.
I didn't know what they were talking about, but I sensed that it's not good, something.
And I remember my father was telling, well, I will take my chances with Germans.
I will take my chances with Germans.
He really was afraid very much of Russians more than he did Germans.
And the war-- the war began.
I don't-- don't remember if that's how it begin, all over Poland, and then Lithuania.
But one Sunday morning, I was playing outside with a group of kids.
And since we lived near the river, there was also a bridge.
So it was like a strategic point.
Well, they concentrated on bombing that bridge, I suppose.
And just-- I tell you, in-- in we heard the shrieking noises of airplanes charging-- all of a sudden.
All of a sudden.
And in a split moment, the whole group of kids-- those were kids, not only Jewish kids.
They were all kids, you know, neighborhood kids that were playing.
In split second, the bomb fell.
The debris was-- I was-- I was lucky.
I run to pick up a ball that the kids threw.
And it was in a little ditch, like-- I was-- I was in the ditch when all this was happening.
It became dark and debris was falling.
Then, it became very quiet.
I was petrified.
I was clutching the ball.
I-- I went out of this ditch.
And it was silent there.
And there was no kids.
There was no-- none.
Nothing was happening.
It-- debris everywhere.
It was awful scene.
It was awful.
And then I hear from far my father's voice.
Bachia, Bachia, Bachia.
And then, I felt somebody grab me.
And that was my father.
And he dragged me out of there.
Carry me out of there.
Later, we found out that most of the kids that were there, they were-- they were no more.
They were killed.
And that was my introduction to-- to-- to war.
Very soon after, our windows were covered with-- with all sorts of things so the light will not go through.
And then my father talked about the-- that we need to go to bankers to find places, you know, in case of alarm.
He was instructing us what to do.
And what they were trying-- they were-- they started to do preparations with food, with-- have enough bread, have enough water.
And I knew that things are-- awful things are about to happen.
And sure enough, not long after this, my father and I were standing on the street and watching German soldiers marching through the-- in the middle of the street.
They were so impressive, I tell you.
And scary-looking, though, but very impressive.
They were marching with a goose step and then singing.
You know the song?
I remember the song.
Oh my goodness, 'The Happy Wanderer,' to the tune of 'Happy Wanderer.'
Whenever I hear now the song, that's what it reminds me.
And I cannot listen to it.
It makes me absolutely-- I-- I just can't.
And it was still playing the song.
They were marching.
Soon after they-- their march, they took over our city.
All sorts of proclamations were announced.
First of all, Jews were-- Jewish kids were not allowed to go to school.
Jews were not allowed to-- to-- to do a lot of things.
Of course, my father was still working.
And that's why I think that he probably tampered with papers and wanted to-- to come as Aryan.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were supposed to wear yellow stars.
And I remember very well how my mom was making those and putting on me and on my sister.
We were not allowed to walk on the sidewalks.
We had to-- we had to walk where the gutter is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not allowed to go to schools, of course.
My sisters stayed home.
There were all sorts of-- it was a campaign to humiliate.
It was campaign to degrade, and to humiliate, to belittle, and to demean.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Very soon afterwards, I can't tell you the dates, you know.
It's so difficult for me, even this is-- you need to look up for-- it's well-documented.
So it shouldn't be very difficult to find the exact dates.
I remember very well march to ghetto.
We were allowed to take only the things that we can carry.
Unfortunately, or fortunately, we did not march with the first group to ghetto because I think my father was managed-- managed to tamper our census, with our papers.
However, it didn't take very long.
And they did find out that we are Jews and there were other people.
So we were gathered in a special place.
And from there, we were marching.
And so we were the last people, the last group, that were marching to ghetto.
And I remember that march very well.
I remember the kids that I used to play with.
I thought that they liked me.
I certainly didn't look different than they.
So they called Jews, Jews.
Jews, keep going.
Keep going, you know.
I didn't know where we were going.
I didn't know that we're going to ghetto.
I thought we're going downtown, someplace.
I didn't know.
Nobody really was explaining or telling us exactly.
My parents wanted to protect us, I suppose, by not telling where we're going.
So the march, it probably wasn't that long, but it seemed to me like forever.
And we came to this place that was with barbed wire.
And there was also a huge fence.
Part of it was brick fence, part of it was-- was wooden fence.
And on top of it, it was barbed wire.
And then-- and then, farther-- farther down, only barbed wire.
Then, we came-- we came to-- to ghetto.
We were taken to our room.
One-- it's an apartment building with many rooms.
And I think it was about three story.
We end up on the third-- on the third floor, in a-- in a small room.
That small room was divided by a curtain because another family lived there.
And the room was small, probably like 15 by 15 maybe.
It was divided.
Two family.
Yeah.
I might not be exactly correct, 15 by 15.
Small.
There was absolutely-- and we-- in the hallway, outside, there was one toilet, lavatory.
And the whole floor, several, several rooms had to share that.
There was no water.
There was no electricity.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how they-- I don't know.
I'm trying to remember how they cooked.
I don't know.
I really still don't remember that.
I don't remember seeing a kitchen.
So where was it?
It had to be some place.
But I don't remember.
Life in ghetto, I don't know how long I was in ghetto.
I-- I think-- I think I was probably somewhere around eight or nine months there.
Maybe a little less.
But I cannot even begin to tell you the-- the awful, awful time that we had there.
First of all, I-- I right away got friendly with a group of kids that were older than I was.
And they were very clever.
They knew how to get out.
And they told me, Bachia, I'll show you how to get out of ghetto.
And I said, so when you get out, what are you going to do then.
Well, we're going to go to marketplace.
We're going to get some food.
We're going to get-- and we're going to get like those kids.
We'll be like those kids outside.
Well, very soon, very soon, since I was the smallest in the group, and the thinnest in the group, well, they dug out a hole beneath the barbed wire.
And I could very easily, you know.
And slowly, slowly, they did, too.

And we-- in a group, sometimes three or four of us, would smuggle out of ghetto and would go to marketplace, and would get some-- you know, we were asking for-- for food, for-- for vegetables and stuff.
And I remember distinctly-- oh, my.
I found-- I found a whole-- a whole package of food stamps, German food stamps for German soldiers.
And we figure out what it was.
First, I didn't know what it was.
But we figure out.
And one of the boys that were in the group, he says, Barbara, see, you go to that store.
They'll give you a lot of food.
Give the-- give the-- and I did.
And it was a store filled with-- with wonderful things.
And only German soldiers and very well-dressed women were there.
And I came with these food stamps.
A German soldier-- I couldn't reach the-- the-- the top, you know.
I just put-- so they-- they started to put so many things there.
Sugar, and god knows what else, and flour, and bread, and everything.
I couldn't carry this.
So one of the soldiers looked at me.
I didn't look like a Jewish child.
So he says he's going to help me to take it home.
What do you do then?
That's when I knew that-- What did you do?
I told him that, no, no, that I'm going to go home and bring my mother.
I'll just take a few things and I'll bring-- clever.
Look at that.
My grandkids wouldn't be able to say that.
No, I was about six years old.
About six.
I-- well, I look at my six-year-old that's going to be-- he wouldn't be able.
Are you kidding?
I don't think so.
I-- so quickly I was thinking.
I told him, no, no, I'm going to get my mother.
[GERMAN] I should be bring in my [GERMAN].
I'll bring my mother.
And I-- and I just grabbed what I could and ran out.
I didn't-- how could I bring him to ghetto?
That would be the end of all of us.
And so that's how-- that's how we were in the beginning.
That was in the beginning.
Then, they very soon found out about this-- that kids were getting out of ghetto and they make sure that it would be more difficult.
They make a children's center in ghetto.
It's sort of-- you would call it probably kindergarten, so nurseries.
The kids were all different ages in that children's center.
There were two ladies, Jewish ladies, that were sort of taking care of us, babysitting or teaching us.
There was not very much of teaching going on.
Although, we did sing some songs there until our parents came from work.
The parents, they were going out of the ghetto to work.
And then, usually, in the evening, they would come to pick us up and to go-- go take us home.
Yes.
My mother was a seamstress and working.
I-- I remember the place because she kept talking about it, [NON-ENGLISH], whatever that is.
I don't know.
[NON-ENGLISH] Some kind of a factory that-- with the abbreviation of [NON-ENGLISH].
What was it?
I don't know.
But that was-- that was the place that she worked.
And my father, I think, dug ditches.
I don't know exactly what he was doing, but he was working also.
And they both came approximately at the same time.
Well, there was one incident that I remember that precipitated the fact that we had to leave ghetto, is that, one day, that when we were in children's home, several buses came.
It looks like-- it looked a little bit like the school buses over here except the color was different.
It was like a brownish-- a brown color.
And the windows were-- the window were taped with like a cardboard or something.
And several of the buses came near the children's center.
And I saw-- I saw several German officers came and talked to the two ladies that were with us in the-- I can't tell you how many children were there.
A lot of kids.
More than a hundred.
They were divided in different groups, you know.
And so-- maybe there were more women.
The women that took care of us, I remember two-- two ladies.
And I remember the Germans talking to-- the Nazis were talking to them.
And after a little bit, after a little bit, one lady started to cry and walked away quickly.
And the other one was calling her and say, gather them fast.
Gather them fast, fast.
Don't talk to them.
Don't tell them.
Don't tell them.
Well, when I heard, don't tell them, then I thought that something bad is about to happen.
So I grabbed my sister.
And, behind that building, there were some bushes.
So I-- I-- just like a premonition, I grabbed my sister and I hid in that-- in that little bit of bushes behind the building.
Well, sure enough, they were getting those kids and they're putting them into the buses.
And they were walking.
The kids, they walked willingly.
They were given some candies or something and walking to the buses.
I was watching.
I was watching.
And then the buses started.
And I thought that they're going to-- they're going to drive away, except they were started and going and going.
And nothing is going away.
And so I didn't-- I didn't know what happened.
I stayed behind that-- behind that-- for a long time with my sister.
Not all the kids went to these-- to these buses.
Later on, a long time afterwards, the buses drove away.
And then--
